data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Revolution Medicines (RVMD) After A 321.4% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• Wondering if Revolution Medicines at around US$152.54 is priced for perfection or still leaves room for value? This article looks at what the current market price may be implying.
  • The stock has seen sharp moves recently, with returns of 57.7% over 7 days, 54.1% over 30 days, 93.0% year to date and 321.4% over the past year. These moves can reshape how investors think about both upside and risk.
  • Recent attention around Revolution Medicines has focused on its position in the pharmaceuticals and biotech space and how investors are reacting to its progress and potential within that sector. That backdrop helps explain why such strong recent returns are drawing in new interest as well as closer scrutiny of the price.
  • Even with this excitement, the company currently holds a valuation score of 2 out of 6. Next up is a look at standard valuation approaches and how they compare with a more complete way to think about value later in the article.

Revolution Medicines scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Revolution Medicines Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value using a required rate of return.

For Revolution Medicines, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at a loss of about US$913.7 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections in the model show free cash flow moving from losses in the coming years to a projected US$1,248.1 million in 2030, with further estimates extending out to 2035 based on those earlier inputs.

Putting all of those projected cash flows together, Simply Wall St’s DCF output arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about US$509.01 per share. Compared with the current share price of around US$152.54, this implies the shares are about 70.0% below that modelled value. Within this framework, the stock is treated as undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Revolution Medicines is undervalued by 70.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Revolution Medicines.

Approach 2: Revolution Medicines Price vs Book

For companies that are still loss making or reinvesting heavily, earnings based metrics such as the P/E ratio can be less helpful. In those cases, balance sheet based measures like the price to book, or P/B, ratio are often used instead, because they compare the share price with the accounting value of the company’s net assets.

Growth expectations and risk still matter for P/B. A higher growth outlook or stronger perceived competitive position can justify investors paying a higher multiple of book value, while higher risk, uncertainty or weaker profitability usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple as a rough guide to what might be considered “normal” for a stock.

Revolution Medicines currently trades at a P/B of 18.53x. That sits well above the Biotechs industry average of about 2.37x and also above the peer group average of 9.53x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a suitable P/B might be once factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks are considered together. Because it aims to tailor the multiple to the company’s own profile instead of relying only on broad peer or industry comparisons, it can give a more tailored view of whether the current P/B looks stretched or conservative. In this case, with no separate Fair Ratio provided to compare against the actual 18.53x, the multiple simply looks high relative to both sector and peers without a clear model based anchor.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/B ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 19 top founder-led companies.

You can then compare this with the current price, with the platform continuously updating those Narratives as new earnings or trial news arrives. This lets you see, for example, how one investor might build a bullish Narrative around a Fair Value near US$170.0, while another takes a more cautious view closer to US$73.0, and decide for yourself which version of the story feels closer to what you think is realistic.

  • Builds in bullish assumptions that revenue reaches US$2.1b by 2029 and earnings move to US$424.4m. The shares are assumed to trade on a P/E of 117.4x, with a 7.1% discount rate used to bring those numbers back to today.

data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Prudential Financial's current share price reflects its true worth, starting with a clear look at valuation can help you decide how it fits in your portfolio.
  • The stock last closed at US$99.42, with returns of 2.9% over 7 days, 6.7% over 30 days, a negative 12.7% year to date, 6.2% over 1 year, 31.5% over 3 years, and 29.1% over 5 years, giving you a mixed picture of recent and longer term performance.
  • Recent moves in Prudential Financial's share price sit against a backdrop of ongoing market discussion around large U.S. insurers and their sensitivity to interest rates and capital requirements. Broader sector headlines about regulation, capital strength, and product risk are an important backdrop for thinking about what investors are currently willing to pay for this stock.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Prudential Financial scores 5 out of 6. The rest of this article breaks down how different valuation approaches arrive at that score, and then finishes with a way to think about valuation that goes beyond any single model.

Prudential Financial delivered 6.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Insurance industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Prudential Financial Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how effectively Prudential Financial turns its equity base into earnings above the return that investors are assumed to require. It compares the return generated on book value to the estimated cost of equity and then capitalizes those “excess” profits into an intrinsic value per share.

For Prudential Financial, book value is estimated at $93.23 per share, with a stable earnings per share estimate of $14.54, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 11 analysts. That implies an average Return on Equity of 13.34%. The model assumes a cost of equity of $8.70 per share, which results in an excess return of $5.84 per share.

The stable book value is projected at $108.97 per share, drawn from weighted future Book Value estimates from 7 analysts. Combining this equity base with the excess return assumptions produces an intrinsic value estimate of about $236.47 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about $99.42, this Excess Returns model suggests the stock is 58.0% undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Prudential Financial is undervalued by 58.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Prudential Financial.

Approach 2: Prudential Financial Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it directly ties what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E ratio usually reflects a mix of expectations and risk. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk tends to align with a lower P/E.

Prudential Financial currently trades on a P/E of 9.78x. That sits below the Insurance industry average P/E of 11.65x and also below the peer average of 16.61x, suggesting the market is applying a lower earnings multiple than these broad benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for Prudential Financial is 12.93x. This is a proprietary view of what the P/E might be given factors such as the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and key risks. Because it is tailored to the company’s own fundamentals rather than broad group averages, it can offer a more company specific reference point than simple peer or industry comparisons.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 12.93x with the actual P/E of 9.78x indicates that, on this metric, Prudential Financial appears undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED
